overview.body: Last week you bolted file I/O onto your road trip game and made progress persist between sessions. That was the BUILD week. This is the ENHANCE week. The destination menus you carried forward from Lab 1.3 are still a wall of if/elif chains. Three menus, dozens of lines, all of them doing the same job in slightly different ways. Parallel lists — the central pattern of Chapter 7 — collapse that wall into a few lines of data and one universal display function. You'll also add tracking lists so the trip report can show the player's actual route and the hazards they faced, and extend save/load so those lists persist across sessions.

mentorQuote.quote: When you find yourself writing the same shape of code three times in a row, that's data wearing a logic costume. The structure is begging to be a list. Once you see it, you can't unsee it — and your code gets so much shorter.

objectives: Replace the destination if/elif chains with parallel lists — three per city: destinations, distances, notes
Add three universal destination helpers to the library: display_destinations(), get_destination(), get_redirect()
Extend save_game() and load_game() to persist both tracking lists using a ---LISTS_END--- separator
Add display_numbered_list() to the library and use it in the Trip Summary
overview.keyConceptsText: Parallel lists with initial values (same index links related items). Indexing with [choice - 1]. Building lists with .append(). Iterating with for i in range(len(lst)). Using in to check membership, .index() to find positions, .remove() and .pop() to remove items, .copy() to avoid mutating originals. Writing lists to files line by line. Reading lists with a separator marker. Refactoring functions from a lab file into the library.
programRequirements: Your program MUST use parallel lists (destinations, distances, notes) for each city
Your program MUST use [choice - 1] indexing to look up destinations and distances
Your program MUST use .append() to build visited_cities and hazards_list
Your saved file MUST use the ---LISTS_END--- marker to separate visited_cities from hazards_list
Your function_library.py destination helpers MUST accept the parallel lists as parameters (no module-level globals from the lab file)
overview.filenameNote: Save your main game file as lab-3-2.py. Your function_library.py from Lab 3.1 grows this week — six functions get added or extended. The save file savegame.txt is generated at runtime; you do not submit it.

dataFiles.format: Your reusable function module. Carries forward from Lab 3.1 with significant growth this week: save_game() and load_game() migrate here from lab-3-1.py, then get extended; four new destination/display helpers are added.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together this week. The big move is splitting your code across two files: lab-specific game flow stays in lab-3-2.py; reusable mechanics (save/load, destination helpers, display helpers) move into function_library.py. The [NEW], [MOVED], [REPLACED], and [EXTENDED] tags show what changes and at which checkpoint.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- IN function_library.py ----
flip_coin(), roll_d20(), check_percentage()     (keep from 2.2)
get_valid_int(), get_yes_no()                   (keep from 2.3)
save_game(...)                                  [MOVED-CP2, EXTENDED-CP8]
load_game()                                     [MOVED-CP2, EXTENDED-CP8]
SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt'                      [MOVED-CP2]
display_destinations(destinations, distances, notes)   [NEW-CP4]
get_destination(destinations, distances, choice)       [NEW-CP5]
get_redirect(destinations, distances, avoid)           [NEW-CP5]
display_numbered_list(items, title)                    [NEW-CP9]

# ---- IN lab-3-2.py ----

# ---- DISTANCE CONSTANTS ----
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150, SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200, ...   (keep from 2.3)
MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10, HAZARD_CHANCE = 30        (keep from 2.3)

# ---- PARALLEL LISTS ----
SA_DESTINATIONS = ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', 'Austin']  [NEW-CP3]
SA_DISTANCES    = [SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, SAT_TO_AUSTIN]  [NEW-CP3]
SA_NOTES        = ['BEACH!', 'Gateway East', 'Keep it Weird']  [NEW-CP3]
HOU_*, AUS_*  (same shape, three lists each)     [NEW-CP6]

def get_city_data(city):                         [NEW-CP6]
│── if city == 'San Antonio': return SA_*
│── elif city == 'Houston':   return HOU_*
│── else (Austin):           return AUS_*

# ---- MAIN MENU LOOP ----   (keep from 3.1)
while in_program:
│── New / Load / Quit menu
│── if Load: function_library.load_game() returns 11-tuple   [EXTENDED-CP8]
│── if New: initialize session accumulators
│
│── # ---- PLAY-AGAIN LOOP ----   (keep from 3.1)
│   while play_again:
│   │── Reset per-game state (unless resumed)
│   │── visited_cities = ['San Antonio']      [NEW-CP7]
│   │── hazards_list = []                     [NEW-CP7]
│   │
│   │── # ---- IN-GAME LOOP ----   (keep from 3.1, with refactors)
│   │   while playing:
│   │   │── destinations, distances, notes = get_city_data(current_city)  [REPLACED-CP6]
│   │   │── num = function_library.display_destinations(...)   [REPLACED-CP4]
│   │   │── choice = get_valid_int(...)
│   │   │── gate = flip_coin()
│   │   │   if HEADS: dest, dist = function_library.get_destination(...)  [REPLACED-CP5]
│   │   │   if TAILS: dest, dist = function_library.get_redirect(...)     [REPLACED-CP5]
│   │   │── Travel loop with hazards
│   │   │   on hazard: hazards_list.append(hazard)    [NEW-CP7]
│   │   │── After leg: current_city = destination
│   │   │   visited_cities.append(current_city)        [NEW-CP7]
│   │   │── If beach: playing = False
│   │   │── If Houston/SA: 3-way Continue/Save/Quit-no-save
│   │   │   on Save: function_library.save_game(..., visited_cities, hazards_list)  [EXTENDED-CP8]
│   │
│   │── After in-game loop:
│   │   ├── if saved_and_quit: skip everything, back to main menu
│   │   ├── Print TRIP SUMMARY
│   │   ├── function_library.display_numbered_list(visited_cities, 'YOUR ROUTE')  [NEW-CP9]
│   │   ├── function_library.display_numbered_list(hazards_list, 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED')  [NEW-CP9]
│   │   ├── Update session accumulators
│   │   └── play_again = get_yes_no('Play again? (y/n): ')
│   │
│   └── After play-again loop:
│       └── if not saved_and_quit: print SESSION STATISTICS
│
└── Back to main menu loop

content.body: A clean starting point for Lab 3.2. You'll copy lab-3-1.py to lab-3-2.py, update the docstring, and confirm everything still runs before refactoring anything. function_library.py carries forward unchanged this checkpoint — you'll start growing it in CP2.

Mixing 'fix the old code' with 'add new code' is the fastest way to break a working program. This week has a lot of refactoring; you can't tell whether a bug is in the refactor or in the copy unless you start from a known-good state. Confirm Lab 3.1 still works in its new filename before touching anything.

items: Copy lab-3-1.py to a new file named lab-3-2.py in the same folder
Do NOT copy function_library.py — it stays the same file, in the same place. You'll just edit it during CP2
Update the docstring at the top of lab-3-2.py: change the title to Lab 3.2: Lists to the Rescue, update the description, and change the chapter list to Chapter 7 Concepts:

Run lab-3-2.py exactly as-is. It should behave identically to Lab 3.1. Play one game, verify the trip summary appears, verify session stats run. If anything has changed, find and fix the copy problem before moving on

content.body: No new BookEx patterns yet — this checkpoint is pure cleanup. CP2 introduces the refactoring lesson; CP3 introduces Program 7-1 (list with initial values).

Run lab-3-2.py end-to-end. Play one full game to the beach. Verify the main menu appears, the trip summary prints with status BEACH WINNER!, and session statistics appear before you return to the main menu. Choose 3 to quit.

rows: Editing lab-3-1.py directly
Always preserve your previous submission. Copy first, then edit the copy.
Touching function_library.py in this CP
Library changes happen in CP2. This CP only renames a file and updates a docstring.
Different output between 3.1 and 3.2 starting state
If your menus look different, your trip summary text differs, or session stats are missing, your copy is incomplete. Diff the two files until they match.

items: A file named lab-3-2.py with an updated docstring
function_library.py unchanged from Lab 3.1
Running lab-3-2.py plays a complete game identical to Lab 3.1

checkpoints.title: Promote save_game and load_game to the Library

content.quote: Last week your save_game and load_game functions did exactly one job in exactly one file. This week we are going to use them from a different file, and next week we will probably use them again. That moves them across an invisible line: from 'helper for this lab' to 'piece of your permanent toolkit.' When code crosses that line, it gets promoted. Not just copied — promoted. We move it, we remove it from where it used to live, and we update every place that called it. That last part is the refactoring lesson.

content.body: A clean refactoring of two functions out of lab-3-1.py and into function_library.py, along with the constant they depend on. You'll touch four things: the function definitions, the constant declaration, the call sites, and any leftover references.

Moving a function is more than copy-paste. Every function has dependencies — constants it reads, modules it imports, functions it calls. When the function moves, those dependencies have to travel with it OR be passed in as parameters. save_game() depends on SAVE_FILE; they move together. Then every call site (in lab-3-2.py) has to be updated to use the new path: function_library.save_game(...) instead of just save_game(...). Miss one call site and you get NameError at runtime. This is the refactoring practice you'll do hundreds of times in your career.

items: Open both files: lab-3-1.py (which you copied as lab-3-2.py in CP1) and function_library.py side by side
In lab-3-2.py, locate the SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt' line, the entire def save_game(...): function block (including its docstring), and the entire def load_game(): function block
In function_library.py, find a good spot for them — right after the existing validation functions is fine. Add a header comment: # SAVE/LOAD FUNCTIONS (Promoted from lab-3-1.py in Lab 3-2)
Move SAVE_FILE to function_library.py (place it near the top of the file with the other constants), then delete it from lab-3-2.py
Move the save_game() function: cut the entire definition (including docstring) from lab-3-2.py and paste it into function_library.py under your new header comment
Move the load_game() function the same way
Update the call sites in lab-3-2.py: search for save_game( and prefix each call with function_library. so it becomes function_library.save_game(. Do the same for load_game()
Check for leftover references: search lab-3-2.py for any remaining SAVE_FILE — that constant lives only in the library now

Run lab-3-2.py. Play through one game, save mid-trip in Houston, quit, restart, load. The behavior should be identical to Lab 3.1 from the user's perspective. The difference is invisible from outside — that's how you know the refactor succeeded

content.body: Program 5-1 (function_demo.py): shows the module.function syntax for calling functions from another file. Program 5-4 (sum_numbers.py): passing parameters to functions. The refactoring practice isn't a BookEx pattern — it's a real-world skill that comes from doing it. Don't worry if it feels like work; it always feels like work.

Run lab-3-2.py. Choose New Game, travel to Houston, choose Save and quit. Verify savegame.txt is created in your folder. Quit the program (choose 3 at main menu). Run again, choose Load Saved Game. Verify you resume from Houston with your saved miles. Finish the trip. Verify the trip summary and session statistics look identical to Lab 3.1's output.

rows: NameError: name 'SAVE_FILE' is not defined
Reference to SAVE_FILE in lab-3-2.py wasn't removed. The constant lives only in the library now.
NameError: name 'save_game' is not defined
Call site still says save_game(...) instead of function_library.save_game(...). Search the whole file.
AttributeError: module 'function_library' has no attribute 'save_game'
The function definition didn't actually land in function_library.py. Open it and check — it might have ended up nested inside another function by accident.
Function works once, then crashes
You may have copied the function instead of moving it — both files have a definition, but the wrong one is being called. Delete the old definition from lab-3-2.py.

items: function_library.py contains SAVE_FILE, save_game(), and load_game()
lab-3-2.py no longer contains these definitions
All call sites use function_library.save_game(...) and function_library.load_game()
Saving and loading still work end-to-end

content.quote: Open lab-3-2.py and look at your San Antonio menu. Three print() statements for the three options. Three if/elif branches to figure out which destination they picked. Three more if/elif branches to redirect on TAILS. That is twelve lines of logic doing what should be three lines of data. This week we cut that down to three lists of three items, and the code that drives the menu becomes a single loop. Magic moment.

content.body: Three parallel lists for San Antonio's destinations: SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, and SA_NOTES. Each list has three items, and items at the same index describe the same destination. You'll declare them at the top of lab-3-2.py in CP3; you'll use them in CP4 and CP5.

Parallel lists are the simplest way to associate related pieces of data. Same index across all three lists = same destination. SA_DESTINATIONS[0] is 'Corpus Christi', SA_DISTANCES[0] is 150, SA_NOTES[0] is 'BEACH!' — together they describe the first destination. Adding a new destination becomes a one-line change to each list. Compare that to the current pattern where you'd add an elif branch, an if branch in the redirect logic, and a menu print statement. Parallel lists scale; if/elif chains don't.

items: Find a spot near the top of lab-3-2.py, after your distance constants and before def main(). Add a comment header: # DESTINATION DATA AS PARALLEL LISTS (NEW in 3.2)
Declare SA_DESTINATIONS as a list with the three destination names in order: ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', 'Austin']
Declare SA_DISTANCES as a list with the three matching distances. Use the named constants you already have: [SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, SAT_TO_AUSTIN]
Declare SA_NOTES as a list with three short note strings, one per destination: ['BEACH!', 'Gateway East', 'Keep it Weird']
Do NOT remove the if/elif chain yet. The lists exist but nothing uses them until CP4. Your code still works exactly like Lab 3.1 right now

content.body: Program 7-1 (list_demo.py): creating lists with initial values. Section 7.2.2: the textbook calls this 'a list with initial values' but it's the most common Python list pattern. Section 7.3: indexing — my_list[0] for first item, my_list[len(my_list) - 1] for last.
Add a temporary debug print right before def main(): to confirm the lists are defined:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 4
print(f'DEBUG: SA destinations = {SA_DESTINATIONS}')
print(f'DEBUG: SA distances    = {SA_DISTANCES}')
print(f'DEBUG: SA notes        = {SA_NOTES}')
print(f'DEBUG: lengths match? '
      f'{len(SA_DESTINATIONS) == len(SA_DISTANCES) == len(SA_NOTES)}')

content.body: Step 2: Run the program. You should see the three lists print out, and the lengths-match line should print True. If you see False, your lists have different lengths and that will cause IndexError in CP4.

content.output:
DEBUG: SA destinations = ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', 'Austin']
DEBUG: SA distances    = [150, 200, 80]
DEBUG: SA notes        = ['BEACH!', 'Gateway East', 'Keep it Weird']
DEBUG: lengths match? True

content.body: Step 3: Delete the DEBUG print lines before moving to CP4.

rows: Lists are different lengths
Every parallel list MUST have the same length. If SA_DESTINATIONS has 3 items but SA_NOTES has 2, code that loops to len(SA_DESTINATIONS) will crash when it tries to read SA_NOTES[2].
Wrong order across the lists

If your destination order doesn't match your distance order, you get '150 miles to Houston' on the screen. Read the lists vertically: 'Corpus Christi, 150 miles, BEACH!' — that's index 0 across all three. Each row of indices = one destination.
Hardcoded distances instead of named constants
Writing SA_DISTANCES = [150, 200, 80] works, but writing SA_DISTANCES = [SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, SAT_TO_AUSTIN] is cleaner — if you ever update a distance, you only change one place.

items: Three lists declared at module level in lab-3-2.py: SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, SA_NOTES
All three lists are length 3 in the same order
Game still plays identically to Lab 3.1 (lists are declared but nothing uses them yet)

content.body: A new library function, display_destinations(destinations, distances, notes), that takes three parallel lists and prints a numbered menu. Then you'll replace the SA if-branch menu printing in lab-3-2.py with a single call to this function.

This is the first time a function in your library takes lists as parameters. The destination lists live in lab-3-2.py, but the function that displays them lives in function_library.py. They communicate through the parameter list: the lab file passes the lists in, the library function does the display work, and neither file needs to know about the other's internal structure. That's exactly the pattern Module 4 will scale up — data in one place, logic in another, parameters as the bridge.

items: Open function_library.py. After the SAVE/LOAD section, add a new section header: # DESTINATION HELPERS (Added in Lab 3-2)
Define display_destinations(destinations, distances, notes) with the signature shown in the skeleton below

Inside the function: print 'Where would you like to go?', then loop with for i in range(len(destinations)): and print each menu line in the format ' N. CITY - DISTANCE miles (NOTE)' using f-string formatting
Return len(destinations) so the caller knows how many options were shown
Now switch to lab-3-2.py. Find the San Antonio menu block in main() — the four print() calls that show 'Where would you like to go?' and the three destination lines
Replace all four prints with a single call: num_choices = function_library.display_destinations(SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, SA_NOTES)
Use num_choices in the get_valid_int call below: function_library.get_valid_int(f'Choice (1-{num_choices}): ', 1, num_choices)
Leave the Houston and Austin menus alone for now. CP6 extends to them. Right now you'll have a hybrid file: SA uses the new library function, Houston and Austin still use print() chains

content.code:
def display_destinations(destinations, distances, notes):
    """
    Display a numbered destination menu using parallel lists.

    Args:
        destinations (list of str): City names to display
        distances (list of int): Miles to each city (same index)
        notes (list of str): Short tag per city (same index)

    Returns:
        int: The number of destinations shown
    """
    # 1. Print the menu prompt: 'Where would you like to go?'
    # 2. Loop with for i in range(len(destinations)):
    #    - Build the line: f'  {i + 1}. {destinations[i]} - {distances[i]} miles ({notes[i]})'
    #    - Print it
    # 3. Return the length of the destinations list
    pass

content.body: Program 7-3 (list_loop.py): looping through a list with for i in range(len(lst)). Section 7.3: indexing with lst[i]. Section 5.3: functions with parameters, returning a value.
Step 1: Save both files. Run lab-3-2.py. Choose New Game. The SA menu should appear with the new format including the notes column:

content.output:
Where would you like to go?
  1. Corpus Christi - 150 miles (BEACH!)
  2. Houston - 200 miles (Gateway East)
  3. Austin - 80 miles (Keep it Weird)

Choice (1-3):

content.body: Step 2: Choose any destination. Verify the rest of the game still works (gate, travel, hazards, trip summary). The Houston menu (if you reach it) will still use the OLD format — that's expected.
Step 3: Save your work. Both files should be runnable as-is.

rows: num_choices is the wrong value (e.g. 0)
The function isn't returning len(destinations). Check the last line of display_destinations.
Menu prints but get_valid_int errors with bad upper bound
You forgot to capture the return value: num_choices = function_library.display_destinations(...). Without the num_choices =, the return is discarded.
IndexError mid-loop
Your parallel lists have different lengths. Run the DEBUG check from CP3 again to verify they're all length 3.
Different output format than expected

The format string is precise: f' {i + 1}. {destinations[i]} - {distances[i]} miles ({notes[i]})'. Two spaces of indent. i + 1 for 1-based numbering. miles spelled exactly. CodeGrade compares byte-for-byte.

items: display_destinations() function defined in function_library.py
Function takes three list parameters, prints the menu, returns the count
SA menu in lab-3-2.py uses the library call
Houston and Austin menus still use the old print() chains (will be updated in CP6)

checkpoints.title: Build get_destination() and get_redirect() in the Library

content.body: Two more library functions: get_destination(destinations, distances, choice) returns the destination and distance for a 1-based menu choice. get_redirect(destinations, distances, avoid) returns a random different destination for TAILS. Then you'll refactor the SA HEADS and TAILS branches to call them.

get_destination() is one line of real work (return lst1[idx], lst2[idx]) but it eliminates an entire if/elif chain at the call site. get_redirect() shows off the list methods from Section 7.5 — .copy(), .index(), .remove(), .pop() — in a real use case. Both functions accept lists as parameters and don't reach into module-level globals; that's what makes them reusable for Houston and Austin in CP6.

items: In function_library.py, under the DESTINATION HELPERS section, add get_destination(destinations, distances, choice)
Inside get_destination: compute index = choice - 1, then return (destinations[index], distances[index]) as a tuple
Right after, add get_redirect(destinations, distances, avoid)

Inside get_redirect: copy both lists with .copy() so you don't mutate the caller's data, then use .index() and .remove()/.pop() to drop the 'avoid' destination, then random.randint(0, len(dest_copy) - 1) to pick from what's left
Now switch to lab-3-2.py. Find your gate-result if/else block (the part that picks a destination based on HEADS or TAILS)
For HEADS: replace the entire SA if/elif chain with destination, distance = function_library.get_destination(SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, choice)

For TAILS: first call wanted, _ = function_library.get_destination(SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, choice) to figure out what they wanted, then call destination, distance = function_library.get_redirect(SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, wanted) to get the redirect. (The _ is the standard way to say 'I don't need this value')
Leave Houston and Austin alone for now. Hybrid file again — SA uses the new helpers, HOU/AUS still use if/elif chains

content.code:
def get_destination(destinations, distances, choice):
    """
    Return the destination and distance for a 1-based menu choice.

    Args:
        destinations (list of str): City names
        distances (list of int): Miles to each city (same index)
        choice (int): 1-based menu choice

    Returns:
        tuple: (destination_name, distance)
    """
    # 1. Convert 1-based choice to 0-based index: index = choice - 1
    # 2. Return (destinations[index], distances[index]) as a tuple
    pass


def get_redirect(destinations, distances, avoid):
    """
    Pick a random destination from the lists, excluding 'avoid'.

    Args:
        destinations (list of str): City names
        distances (list of int): Miles to each city (same index)
        avoid (str): The destination to exclude

    Returns:
        tuple: (destination_name, distance) for some city != avoid
    """
    # 1. Make a copy of each list (use .copy()) so you don't mutate the
    #    caller's lists
    # 2. If avoid is in the destinations copy:
    #    - Find its index with .index()
    #    - Remove it from destinations copy with .remove(avoid)
    #    - Remove the matching distance with dist_copy.pop(idx)
    # 3. If the copy still has items:
    #    - Pick a random index: random.randint(0, len(dest_copy) - 1)
    #    - Return (dest_copy[idx], dist_copy[idx])
    # 4. Otherwise (avoid was the only option):
    #    - Return (destinations[0], distances[0]) as a fallback
    pass

content.body: Section 7.5.2: .index(item) returns the position of an item. Section 7.5.3: .remove(item) deletes the first matching item. Section 7.5.6: .copy() makes an independent copy of a list. Section 5.6: functions returning multiple values via tuples; the caller unpacks them with a, b = func().

Step 1: Run lab-3-2.py. Start a New Game and pick Houston (option 2). Depending on the gate flip you'll either head to Houston (HEADS) or get redirected to Corpus Christi or Austin (TAILS). Either way the game should continue normally.

Step 2: Run several games to exercise both branches. The new behavior should be functionally identical to Lab 3.1 — same destinations, same distances, same gate-redirect logic — the difference is entirely under the hood.
Step 3: If you'd like to verify get_redirect isn't mutating the originals, add a debug print before/after the call:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 6
print(f'DEBUG before: SA_DESTINATIONS = {SA_DESTINATIONS}')
destination, distance = function_library.get_redirect(
    SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, wanted)
print(f'DEBUG after:  SA_DESTINATIONS = {SA_DESTINATIONS}')

content.body: Both lines should print the original list. If they don't match, you forgot to .copy() inside get_redirect and you're mutating the caller's data.

rows: TAILS always redirects to the same destination
You forgot random.randint, OR you copied the wrong list and got stuck with one option. Walk through get_redirect with destinations = ['Corpus', 'Houston', 'Austin'] and avoid = 'Houston' on paper.
After a few games, the SA menu only shows 1 option
get_redirect is mutating SA_DESTINATIONS directly. Add .copy() on the first two lines.
ValueError: 'X' is not in list
You're calling .remove(avoid) without checking if avoid in dest_copy first. Or you passed the wrong city name string.
TAILS branch crashes when SA only has 1 destination (later)

After removing the only option, your random pick crashes. Use the fallback else branch returning a default. (This won't matter in 3-2 since SA always has 3+ options, but the safety check is good practice.)

items: get_destination() and get_redirect() defined in function_library.py
Both functions accept lists as parameters; neither references module-level globals
get_redirect() uses .copy() so it doesn't mutate caller data
SA branch in lab-3-2.py uses both helpers; Houston and Austin still use old if/elif chains

checkpoints.title: Extend Parallel Lists to Houston and Austin

content.body: Two more triples of parallel lists — one for Houston, one for Austin — plus a small dispatch function get_city_data(city) that returns the right triple for whatever city you're in. Then you'll replace the remaining if-branch menus and destination/redirect logic for HOU and AUS so all three cities use the same library helpers.

The pattern from CPs 3-5 generalizes here. Each city needs three lists; one tiny dispatch function decides which set to use. Everything downstream — menu display, destination lookup, gate redirect — uses the same library code regardless of which city you're in. This is where the file shrinks dramatically: what was a 90-line if/elif wall in 3.1 becomes about 20 lines of data plus a few library calls.

items: Below the SA lists in lab-3-2.py, declare the Houston lists: HOU_DESTINATIONS = ['Galveston', 'San Antonio'], HOU_DISTANCES = [HOU_TO_GALVESTON, HOU_TO_SAN_ANTONIO], HOU_NOTES = ['BEACH!', 'Back West']
Below those, declare the Austin lists: AUS_DESTINATIONS = ['San Antonio'], AUS_DISTANCES = [SAT_TO_AUSTIN], AUS_NOTES = ['Back Home'] (Austin only has one option — back home)

Define the dispatch function get_city_data(city) at module level (above main()) — it takes a city name and returns a tuple of (destinations, distances, notes). Use an if/elif/else to pick the right triple

Why does this stay in lab-3-2.py? Because the city names and the constants SA_*, HOU_*, AUS_* are lab-specific data, not universal logic. The library doesn't know about San Antonio or Houston; it knows about lists

Now refactor the in-game loop's menu logic. Replace the entire if current_city == 'San Antonio': ... elif current_city == 'Houston': ... else (Austin): ... menu-printing block with one block of code that calls get_city_data first, then display_destinations on the result
Same for the HEADS branch: one call to function_library.get_destination(destinations, distances, choice)
Same for the TAILS branch: wanted, _ = function_library.get_destination(...) followed by function_library.get_redirect(destinations, distances, wanted)

After this CP your in-game loop should have NO city-specific if/elif for destinations. The only city-name comparisons left should be checking for beach cities (Corpus Christi, Galveston) and for Austin (which still triggers a 'return to San Antonio?' prompt)

content.body: No new BookEx patterns — this CP applies the patterns from CPs 3-5 to additional data. The mental shift is recognizing that 'add another city' now means 'add another triple of parallel lists and one elif in get_city_data' rather than 'add another full menu printing block, another destination lookup, and another redirect block.'
Step 1: Run a New Game. Travel to Houston (option 2 from SA). When you arrive, verify the Houston menu now shows the notes column:

content.output:
Where would you like to go?
  1. Galveston - 50 miles (BEACH!)
  2. San Antonio - 200 miles (Back West)

Choice (1-2):

content.body: Step 2: Travel through Austin too — from SA, choose Austin. The Austin menu should show one option: 1. San Antonio - 80 miles (Back Home)
Step 3: Play one full game through to the beach. The trip summary should still appear and look the same as before.

rows: Houston menu still shows old format
You didn't refactor the Houston branch — the old elif current_city == 'Houston': menu print is still there. Delete it; all three cities now use the same code via get_city_data.
NameError: name 'HOU_DESTINATIONS' is not defined
get_city_data references the lists before they're declared. Check that all the list declarations appear at the top of the file, before get_city_data.
Austin menu shows 0 options
Your AUS_DESTINATIONS declaration is empty [] instead of ['San Antonio']. Austin still has one option (back home).
Reaching Austin loops forever

After arriving in Austin, the game asks 'Return to San Antonio? (y/n):'. That logic is separate from the menu and should still work. If the menu shows but the question doesn't, check that you didn't accidentally delete the Austin special-case block.

items: All three triples of parallel lists declared at module level
get_city_data(city) dispatch function returns the right triple
In-game loop has no city-specific if/elif for destinations, gates, or redirects
All three cities' menus now show the notes column
Full game play-through still works

checkpoints.title: Add Tracking Lists: visited_cities and hazards_list

content.quote: The route is the story. The hazards are the war stories. When the player finishes their trip, what they want to see isn't 'You traveled 450 miles' — they want to see 'San Antonio, then Houston, then Galveston, and Buc-ee's tried to get me but I escaped.' We are not tracking statistics this checkpoint. We are tracking memories. Two lists, appended to as the game runs, become the trip report your players actually care about.

content.body: Two empty lists, initialized once per game: visited_cities starts with ['San Antonio'] (where you begin) and hazards_list starts empty. Each time you arrive somewhere new, append the destination to visited_cities. Each time a hazard fires, append the hazard string to hazards_list. By end of game, both lists tell the story of the trip.

.append() is the fundamental list-building operation. You start with an empty (or near-empty) list, and grow it one item at a time as new things happen. The trick is initializing in the right scope: these lists belong to a single game, so they live inside the play-again loop and reset on each new game. If you initialize them outside, they accumulate across games (and that's a fun feature but not what we want today).

items: Find the per-game reset block inside the play-again loop in main(). It currently resets total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped when not resumed_game
Add two new resets inside that if not resumed_game: block: visited_cities = ['San Antonio'] and hazards_list = []
Find the hazard block in the travel loop — the spot where a hazard fires and you print its name. Immediately after print(hazard), add hazards_list.append(hazard)
Find the city-arrival code right after the travel loop ends — where you set current_city = destination. Add visited_cities.append(current_city) on the next line

Handle the Austin special case: when Austin returns to SA, you also need to append SA to visited_cities (otherwise the route shows 'San Antonio, Austin' and then jumps to Houston without recording the trip back to SA in between)

Update function_library.load_game() return value handling in main(): when the Load branch succeeds, you'll need visited_cities and hazards_list from the loaded tuple. For now, since the library function still returns a 9-tuple, just initialize empty lists in the Load branch too: visited_cities = []; hazards_list = []. CP8 will extend the library to return them properly

content.body: Program 7-2 (list_demo2.py): empty list plus .append(). Section 7.2.1: the pattern of starting with [] and growing as you go. Section 7.7: lists are frequently used as accumulators — same pattern as your numeric accumulators (total_miles += ...), but you're accumulating strings instead of numbers.
Step 1: Add a DEBUG print at the end of main()'s game logic to see what was tracked:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 8
print(f'DEBUG: visited_cities = {visited_cities}')
print(f'DEBUG: hazards_list = {hazards_list}')

content.body: Step 2: Play a full game through to Galveston. Verify visited_cities shows the route in order, and hazards_list shows every hazard that fired (regardless of whether you escaped). Example:

content.output:
DEBUG: visited_cities = ['San Antonio', 'Houston', 'Galveston']
DEBUG: hazards_list = ["Buc-ee's appears!"]

content.body: Step 3: Try a game where you visit Austin and return to SA. Verify visited_cities shows the return: ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'San Antonio', ...]

Step 4 (round-trip check): Play a game that visits Austin and returns. From San Antonio choose Austin (option 3); when prompted, choose y to return to San Antonio; then choose Houston (option 2); when you arrive in Houston, choose option 2 (Save and quit). Now restart and Load. Verify that the loaded visited_cities contains ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'San Antonio', 'Houston'] in that order. Lab 3.3 will calculate unique-city counts from this list, so the duplicates AND the order matter.
Step 5: Delete the DEBUG print before CP8. The Trip Summary in CP9 will display these lists properly.

rows: visited_cities is empty at end of game
You initialized it outside the play-again loop, OR you forgot to .append(current_city) after the travel loop ends. Trace the code.
visited_cities persists across replays
You initialized it OUTSIDE the play-again loop. It needs to be inside, in the per-game reset block, so each new game starts fresh.
Hazards appear in the list even when escaped
That's correct — hazards_list records every hazard encountered, not just the ones you got delayed by. Escape status is tracked separately via hazards_escaped.
Route shows Austin once but skips the return to SA

You appended destination in the travel loop, but the Austin special-case sets current_city = 'San Antonio' separately without appending. Add an explicit visited_cities.append('San Antonio') in the Austin branch.

items: visited_cities initialized with ['San Antonio'] in the per-game reset
hazards_list initialized empty in the per-game reset
.append() calls when arriving at any city
.append() call when a hazard fires (before the escape coin flip)
Austin branch appends SA back to visited_cities on return
Lists persist correctly during one game and reset for new games

checkpoints.title: Extend save_game and load_game to Persist the Lists

content.quote: Every new piece of state you save is another thing that has to survive load. The number-one bug in save systems is fields that work in isolation but break under round-trip. Save with 3 hazards, load, save again, load again — do you still have 3 hazards? Do you have 6 because they doubled? Do you have 0 because they vanished? Test the round trip after every extension. I have seen save systems ship to production where the first load worked perfectly and the second load wiped the player's inventory. Round trip, every time.

content.body: Extended versions of save_game() and load_game() in function_library.py. save_game grows from 9 parameters to 11 (adding visited_cities and hazards_list). The save file grows by however many cities and hazards exist, plus a ---LISTS_END--- marker between them. load_game reads back the 9 base fields, then loops to collect cities until it hits the marker, then loops to collect hazards until end-of-file. Returns an 11-tuple.

Variable-length data in files is harder than fixed-length data. You can't say 'read line 10' because you don't know how many cities there are. The solution is the separator pattern: write a marker the data would never accidentally contain, and read until you see it. ---LISTS_END--- can't be a city name (the city list ends when you hit the marker) and can't be a hazard name (the hazard list ends at end-of-file). This pattern scales: Module 4's data files use a similar idea with comma delimiters and end-of-file.

items: Open function_library.py and find your save_game() function
Change the signature: add visited_cities and hazards_list as the last two parameters (after session_hazards). Update the docstring to describe them
After writing the 9 base fields, add a for loop: for city in visited_cities: outfile.write(city + '\n')
Write the marker line: outfile.write('---LISTS_END---\n')
After the marker, write another loop for hazards: for hazard in hazards_list: outfile.write(hazard + '\n')
Now find load_game() in the library and extend it
After reading the 9 base fields, add the visited_cities loop: visited_cities = [], then read first line with rstrip('\n'), then while line != '---LISTS_END---' and line != '': append and read next
Then add the hazards loop: hazards_list = [], read next line with rstrip('\n'), then while line != '': append and read next
Update the return statement to include both lists: return (..., visited_cities, hazards_list) (11 values)
Now switch to lab-3-2.py. Find the call to function_library.save_game in your mid-trip Save & quit branch. Add visited_cities, hazards_list as the last two arguments
Find the call to function_library.load_game() in the Load main-menu branch. Update the tuple unpack to expect 11 values, ending with ..., visited_cities, hazards_list = saved
Remove the placeholder empty-list initialization you added in CP7 for the Load branch — the loaded values now come from the file

content.code:
# In function_library.py — UPDATED SIGNATURE
def save_game(player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns,
              total_hazards, total_escaped, games_played,
              session_miles, session_hazards,
              visited_cities, hazards_list):
    """Save full session state to a file."""
    # 1. Open SAVE_FILE in 'w' mode (use 'outfile = open(...)')
    # 2. Write the 9 base fields as before (with str() and '\n')
    # 3. Loop through visited_cities, writing each city + '\n'
    # 4. Write the marker line: '---LISTS_END---\n'
    # 5. Loop through hazards_list, writing each hazard + '\n'
    # 6. Close the file
    # 7. Print 'Game saved!'
    pass


def load_game():
    """Load full session state. Returns 11-tuple, or None."""
    try:
        # 1. Open SAVE_FILE in 'r' mode
        # 2. Read the 9 base fields as before
        # 3. Loop to read visited_cities:
        #    - visited_cities = []
        #    - Read first line, rstrip('\n')
        #    - while line != '---LISTS_END---' and line != '':
        #        - append, read next line
        # 4. Loop to read hazards_list:
        #    - hazards_list = []
        #    - Read next line, rstrip('\n')
        #    - while line != '':
        #        - append, read next line
        # 5. Close the file
        # 6. Return 11-tuple: 9 base fields + 2 lists
        pass
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return None

content.body: Program 7-13 (write_list.py): writing a list to a file with a loop. Program 7-14 (read_list.py): reading a list from a file with readline() in a loop until empty string. Section 6.4 (sentinel reading): using a known marker string to signal end of one section, which is exactly what ---LISTS_END--- does.
Step 1: Save round-trip. Start a New Game, travel to Houston, save and quit. Open savegame.txt in Notepad. Verify the format:

content.output:
TestPlayer
Houston
300
2
1
1
0
0
0
San Antonio
Houston
---LISTS_END---
Buc-ee's appears!

content.body: Step 2: Load round-trip. Quit the program, restart, choose Load Saved Game. Verify you resume from Houston AND that visited_cities and hazards_list retain their saved values (use a temporary DEBUG print to inspect them if needed).

Step 3: Double-save round-trip. T-Bone's actual test: load, travel a bit more, save again, quit, restart, load. The lists should have grown but not duplicated. If your cities show up twice, your load is appending to existing lists instead of replacing them.

Step 4: Finish a game to the beach, decline replay, quit, restart, choose Load. There's no save file (the game was completed, no mid-trip save). Verify the fallback message: 'No saved game found. Starting new game.'

rows: Loaded game has empty visited_cities
The load loop is exiting immediately. Either you forgot the first readline() before the while check, or the marker is being mistaken for a city name. Print the line at top of loop to debug.
Loaded game has the marker AS a city
Your while condition is just line != ''. It needs to ALSO check line != '---LISTS_END---'.
Extra blank line between cities in saved file
You're writing '\n' twice per city, or your city strings already had a trailing newline when appended. .rstrip('\n') on load handles incoming newlines.
Tuple unpack fails with 'too many values to unpack' on load
load_game now returns 11 values but the caller is unpacking 9. Update the unpack site in main().

items: save_game() in library: 11 parameters; writes 9 base lines + cities + marker + hazards
load_game() in library: returns 11-tuple; uses marker to separate the two list reads
Save round-trip preserves both lists
Load fallback (no file) still returns None
Call sites in lab-3-2.py pass and unpack 11 values

checkpoints.title: Add display_numbered_list and Use It in the Trip Summary

content.body: One more library function: display_numbered_list(items, title). Then you'll use it twice in the Trip Summary — once for visited_cities (route) and once for hazards_list (hazards encountered). The Trip Summary becomes the player's story of their trip.

Numbered lists with a title and a divider are a common output pattern. You wrote one manually in CP7 to debug; now you write it once in the library and call it everywhere. The Trip Summary will use it for two lists this lab, and Lab 3.3 will use it for at least one more (sorted hazards). Three uses across two labs — that's the bar for promoting a function to the library.

items: Open function_library.py. After the destination helpers section, add a new header: # DISPLAY HELPERS (Added in Lab 3-2)
Define display_numbered_list(items, title)

Inside the function: print the title, then print a divider line made of '-' * len(title) dashes (exactly the same length as the title), then loop with for i in range(len(items)) printing each item as ' N. ITEM', then print a blank line
Now switch to lab-3-2.py and find the Trip Summary block in main() — after the print('Status: ...') lines and before the play-again prompt
Replace any existing YOUR ROUTE print logic with a single call: function_library.display_numbered_list(visited_cities, 'YOUR ROUTE')

For hazards, handle the empty case specially: if len(hazards_list) > 0: call display_numbered_list(hazards_list, 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED'); else print a small 'None - Lucky traveler!' block with the same title/divider/blank-line shape
Verify the Trip Summary order: Driver, Final City, totals, status, blank, YOUR ROUTE block, HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED block, divider, blank, then play-again prompt

content.code:
def display_numbered_list(items, title):
    """
    Display a list with a title, an underline, and 1-based numbering.

    Args:
        items (list): Items to display
        title (str): Title to show above the list
    """
    # 1. Print the title
    # 2. Print a divider line: '-' multiplied by len(title)
    # 3. Loop with for i in range(len(items)):
    #    - Print f'  {i + 1}. {items[i]}'
    # 4. Print a blank line at the end
    pass

content.body: Program 7-3 again, this time as a library function. Section 5.2: functions that have side effects (printing) but don't return anything — they're called for the work they do, not the value they produce. display_numbered_list is a side-effect-only function, like Python's built-in print().
Step 1: Run a full game to the beach. When the trip summary prints, you should see two numbered lists like this:

content.output:
YOUR ROUTE
----------
  1. San Antonio
  2. Houston
  3. Galveston

HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED
-------------------
  1. Buc-ee's appears!

content.body: Step 2: Run a game with no hazards (you'll need RNG luck). Verify the HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED block shows the 'None - Lucky traveler!' message instead of an empty numbered list.
Step 3: Visit Austin and return to SA before reaching the beach. Verify visited_cities shows all five entries: ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'San Antonio', 'Houston', 'Galveston'].

rows: Divider is wrong length
'-' * len(title) matches the title length exactly. If your divider is too short, you probably hardcoded a number; if too long, you might be doing '-' * 50.
Empty hazard list shows '0. ' or no list at all
Check the if len(hazards_list) > 0: guard. The else branch should still print SOMETHING that tells the player there were no hazards.
Trip summary order looks different from expected output
The order is fixed: header divider, summary fields, blank, ROUTE block, HAZARDS block, closing divider. Check your existing print statements haven't drifted.
Numbered list prints right after the previous line with no space
display_numbered_list should print a trailing blank line for separation. Add a print() at the end.

items: display_numbered_list(items, title) in function_library.py
Function prints title, divider, numbered items, trailing blank line
Trip Summary in lab-3-2.py uses the helper for visited_cities
Trip Summary uses the helper for hazards_list (with empty-case fallback)
Output matches the canonical Trip Summary block in the final check

beforeYouSubmit: lab-3-2.py has the three triples of parallel lists at module level (SA, HOU, AUS)
lab-3-2.py has NO city-specific if/elif for destinations — all menus go through the library helpers
visited_cities tracks the route, including any return trips through SA
hazards_list tracks every hazard encountered, regardless of escape
Both files have updated docstrings with name, abc123, section, and description
Save/load round trip preserves all 11 fields including both lists
function_library.py destination helpers accept lists as parameters (no module-level globals from lab-3-2.py)
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–7 only. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, loops, functions, function_library, file I/O, try-except)
Multiple return values via tuples; unpacking at call site
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Dictionaries, sets, list comprehensions, readlines(), with statement, or any Chapter 8+ techniques. Note: with is in Chapter 6 but we're staying with explicit open()/close() through Module 3 for consistency.

finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Lab 3.3 builds the Master capstone of Module 3 on top of this. function_library.py grows again next week.

finalCheck.expectedOutputNote: This run uses inputs 1 (New Game) → TestPlayer → 2 (Houston) → 1 (Continue journey) → 1 (Galveston) → n (no replay) → 3 (Quit) with the deterministic CodeGrade fixture. With real random, your specific rolls and hazards will differ — but the structure must match: main menu → new game → play one game (with the new parallel-list menus and Trip Summary numbered lists) → session stats → main menu → quit.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: NameError: 'save_game' is not defined or similar after CP2
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 — one or more call sites still say save_game(...) instead of function_library.save_game(...). Search lab-3-2.py for any bare save_game or load_game references.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Menu still shows old format without the notes column at one or more cities
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 and CP6 — you may have refactored SA but not the Houston or Austin menu. After CP6, all three cities should go through function_library.display_destinations(...).

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: TAILS gate always sends the player to the same destination
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP5 — get_redirect needs random.randint AND needs .copy() on both lists. Without copy, you mutate the originals and eventually only one option remains.
Check CP7 — you forgot the .append(current_city) after the travel loop ends. The list is initialized but nothing's adding to it.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: too many values to unpack error after loading a saved game
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP8 — you extended load_game to return 11 values, but the unpack site in main() still expects 9. Update the unpack to include visited_cities and hazards_list.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Save file shows the ---LISTS_END--- marker but loading doesn't find it
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP8 — your while condition in load_game probably just checks line != ''. It also needs line != '---LISTS_END---'.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip Summary lists are missing the divider line or trailing space
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP9 — display_numbered_list needs to print: title, then '-' * len(title), then the numbered items, then a blank print() at the end.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Lists persist across games (e.g., game 2 shows game 1's hazards too)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP7 — the list initializations need to be INSIDE the play-again loop's per-game reset block, not before the loop.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Loaded game crashes with ValueError or wrong field counts
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP8 — if your save file is from before you finished CP8 (or from Lab 3.1 directly), it has the wrong number of lines for the new format. Delete savegame.txt and start fresh. The library's load_game() handles missing files cleanly, but a partial or old-format file may have parse errors. If a fresh save then loads cleanly, the fix is good. If it still crashes, your load_game() isn't catching ValueError — update the except clause to except (FileNotFoundError, ValueError):.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 7-1 (lists with initial values), 7-3 (list iteration), and 7-7/7-8 (in operator, index method, remove method) — these four show every list operation you need this week
When refactoring (especially CP2 and CP6), make ONE change at a time and re-run after each. Don't refactor SA, Houston, and Austin all at once — do them one at a time
If a library function isn't working, test it in isolation. Open function_library.py, add temporary code at the bottom under if __name__ == '__main__': that calls the function with known inputs
For CP8 (extending save/load), do the SAVE side first and inspect savegame.txt in Notepad before touching the LOAD side. Half the load bugs are actually save bugs from the previous step
Submit to CodeGrade early — the grader tests function_library.py in isolation, so you'll know immediately if a library function is broken without playing through a full game
Come to Zoom or office hours — debugging across two files is much easier with someone watching the screen
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 7 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example, your primary reference
